place_value_1.label: Place Value (Tens & Ones)
misconceptions: Treating each digit independently (42 = 4 and 2, not 40 and 2)
place_value_1.advanced: Counts, reads, and writes numbers to 120. Understands that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones. Uses place value understanding to compare and order numbers using >, <, =. Adds and subtracts multiples of 10 mentally.
place_value_1.proficient: Represents numbers to 120 using objects, drawings, and expanded/standard form. Identifies the tens and ones in a two-digit number. Compares two two-digit numbers using place value.
place_value_1.developing: Counts to 100 with some errors beyond 50. Identifies tens and ones with concrete models (base-ten blocks) but struggles with symbolic representation. Compares numbers with support.
place_value_1.beginning: Counts to 40–50 with errors. Does not yet reliably distinguish tens from ones. Needs base-ten blocks and significant scaffolding to represent two-digit numbers.

addition_subtraction_1.label: Addition & Subtraction within 20
misconceptions: Equal sign means "the answer comes next"
Subtraction is only "take away" (not comparison or missing addend)
addition_subtraction_1.advanced: Adds and subtracts within 20 using mental strategies (doubles, near doubles, make 10, count on). Solves all problem types (result unknown, change unknown, start unknown). Writes and explains equations using the equal sign correctly.
addition_subtraction_1.proficient: Adds and subtracts within 20 using objects, drawings, or strategies (counting on, making 10). Solves one-step word problems for result-unknown and change-unknown types. Understands the meaning of the equal sign.
addition_subtraction_1.developing: Adds and subtracts within 10 reliably; struggles with teen numbers. Uses counting-all strategy (not yet counting on). Solves result-unknown word problems with manipulatives.
addition_subtraction_1.beginning: Adds within 5 using fingers or objects with some accuracy. Cannot yet subtract reliably. Needs direct modeling to connect word problems to operations.

geometry_1.label: Geometry & Spatial Reasoning
misconceptions: Shapes must be in standard orientation to be identified
Halves must look identical (ignoring equivalent area with different shapes)
geometry_1.advanced: Distinguishes defining vs. non-defining attributes of shapes. Composes and decomposes 2-D and 3-D shapes to create new shapes. Partitions circles and rectangles into halves and fourths, names the parts, and understands the whole is two or four of the parts.
geometry_1.proficient: Identifies and describes 2-D shapes by their attributes (number of sides, corners). Builds and draws shapes with specified attributes. Partitions circles and rectangles into two or four equal shares and describes them as halves/fourths.
geometry_1.developing: Names common 2-D shapes. Identifies the number of sides or corners with support. Partitions shapes into halves when shown a model but struggles with fourths.
geometry_1.beginning: Recognizes basic shapes (circle, square) but cannot describe their attributes. Cannot yet partition shapes into equal parts without direct modeling.

measurement_1.label: Measurement, Time & Data
misconceptions: Gaps or overlaps when iterating units invalidate measurement
Clock hands are interchangeable (hour vs. minute)
measurement_1.advanced: Measures lengths using same-size units without gaps or overlaps and expresses the measurement as a number. Orders three or more objects by length. Tells time to the hour and half hour on analog and digital clocks. Organizes, represents, and interprets data with up to three categories.
measurement_1.proficient: Measures length by laying multiple copies of a shorter object end to end. Compares the lengths of two objects using a third object. Tells time to the hour and half-hour. Collects and organizes data into categories and reads simple graphs.
measurement_1.developing: Compares two objects directly (longer/shorter). Uses non-standard units with some gaps/overlaps. Tells time to the hour on a digital clock. Participates in data collection but needs help reading graphs.
measurement_1.beginning: Uses vague language for length comparison. Cannot yet iterate measurement units. Does not reliably read an analog clock. Needs significant support with data activities.

data_1.label: Data & Graphs
misconceptions: The height of a bar tells "which is better" rather than "how many"
Tallies must be in groups of 5 only
data_1.advanced: Collects data, creates tally charts and picture/bar-type graphs independently. Answers comparison questions (how many more, how many fewer) using the data. Generates original questions from a data display.
data_1.proficient: Collects, sorts, and organizes data into up to three categories. Reads tally charts and picture/bar-type graphs to answer simple questions. Draws conclusions from data displays.
data_1.developing: Sorts data into given categories and counts with support. Reads individual values from a picture graph but struggles with comparison questions.
data_1.beginning: Participates in data sorting with direct guidance. Cannot yet independently read or interpret simple graphs.
